title: Higher singularities in positive characteristic
speaker: Jakub Witaszek
abstract: I will start by reviewing various Hodge-theoretic invariants of complex singularities. After that, I will discuss how to study such singularities using methods of positive characteristic such as Cartier operators. This is based on joint work with Tatsuro Kawakami.

proof_stages.label: Frobenius liftability as rare Hodge-regularity
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines Frobenius liftability modulo p^2, explains its rarity through conjectural toric/ordinary-abelian structure and known cases, and introduces Bott vanishing as the Hodge-theoretic obstruction behind hypersurface rigidity.

proof_stages.label: Singular Frobenius-liftability and the hypersurface target theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker moves from smooth varieties to singularities, formulates the expectation that Frobenius-liftable affine singularities with mild rationality assumptions should be finite images of toroidal singularities, and states the motivating theorem that certain complex hypersurfaces with Frobenius-liftable reductions must be smooth.

proof_stages.label: Higher Du Bois singularities in characteristic zero
proof_stages.summary: Using Hodge decomposition and mixed Hodge theory, the talk introduces Du Bois complexes and defines k-Du Bois and pre-k-Du Bois singularities as conditions that these complexes have the expected low-degree behavior.

proof_stages.label: F-injectivity as the zeroth positive-characteristic analogue
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls F-injective singularities via Frobenius acting injectively on local cohomology and the theorem that Du Bois singularities over characteristic zero are detected by F-injectivity of infinitely many reductions, thereby posing the problem of higher F-injective analogues.

proof_stages.label: Inverse Cartier operators as higher Frobenius maps
proof_stages.summary: Cartier theory is reviewed to construct the inverse Cartier map on differential forms, interpret it as a higher Frobenius, and prove that Frobenius liftability splits this map, hence forces the required local-cohomology injectivity.

proof_stages.label: Definition of k-F-injectivity through perfected reflexive forms
proof_stages.summary: Under normality and rationality hypotheses, k-F-injectivity is defined by local-cohomology injectivity of reflexified inverse Cartier maps in the range i+j≤dim X, equivalently via a colimit object omega^i_perf, and this is compared with a local-cohomology characterization of pre-k-Du Bois singularities.

proof_stages.label: Comparison theorem between k-Du Bois and k-F-injective reductions
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that, assuming the needed ordinary/Bloch-Kato-type input in one direction, k-Du Bois singularities over C are equivalent to k-F-injectivity for infinitely many reductions; the right-to-left proof is sketched by factoring the Du Bois comparison map through the perfected differentials and transferring injectivity.

proof_stages.label: Application to Frobenius-liftable hypersurfaces
proof_stages.summary: The motivating hypersurface theorem is proved by combining Frobenius-liftability ⇒ split inverse Cartier ⇒ k-F-injectivity, the comparison theorem ⇒ k-Du Bois in characteristic zero, and a Hodge-module/D-module criterion saying that a sufficiently high-codimension hypersurface singularity with the relevant higher Du Bois and reflexivity properties is smooth.
